steps.title: Build a Rock Paper Scissors game

narration.default: Today's project: a game where you play Rock Paper Scissors against the computer. Three new ideas in one tutorial — random.choice for picking from a list, multiple elifs to cover the rules, and the and operator (you met it in Boolean Logic) for asking two questions at once. You already know everything else.

Type the very first line:

import random

random is a standard Python module that adds dice-roll-like helpers. import means "load this set of tools so I can use them". After this line, anything inside random becomes available — the most useful one for games is random.choice.

Type:

print("Rock Paper Scissors")

player = input("Enter rock, paper, or scissors: ")

The player variable holds whatever the kid types — text. We won't wrap with int because we're comparing words, not numbers.

Type:

choices = ["rock", "paper", "scissors"]
computer = random.choice(choices)

print("Computer chose:", computer)

choices is a list of the three options. random.choice(choices) picks ONE at random — like rolling a 3-sided die labelled rock/paper/scissors. The print line shows the kid what the computer picked, so they can see whether they won.

steps.title: First check — was it a tie?

narration.default:
Type:

if player == computer:
    print("It's a tie")

If both picked the same thing, it's a tie — easiest case to check first. Putting the simplest case first keeps the elif chain that follows clear.

steps.title: else — everything left is a loss

narration.default:
Type:

else:
    print("You lose")

If none of the if/elif branches matched, it's a loss. else is the catch-all. With nine possible (player, computer) combinations and 4 branches above (1 tie + 3 wins), this else covers exactly the 5 losing cases — neat.

Click Run. Type rock. The terminal prints what the computer chose and one of: It's a tie, You win, or You lose. Run it five or six times — random.choice will pick a different option most times, so each run feels new.

Three new things this tutorial: import random, random.choice, and an elif chain with and. With these you can build dice games, magic 8-balls, random Mad Libs, or a coin flipper. Next up, you'll learn to put the WHOLE game inside a while loop so the kid can play multiple rounds without re-running.
